Failure: only Step 1 (Requirements); recovery is to ask one fast question ('payments in scope?') and accept the rest of the entities as-is since the candidate has already committed to them on the whiteboard.

No failure — the candidate is being thorough; recovery is to push back and explain that more entities is always better than fewer, since under-modeling loses information that re-emerges later as bugs.
questions.explanation: Two-step failure: skipping Step 1 (Requirements) cascades into Step 2 (Entities) because the entity filter ('maintains state or enforces rules') needs a *scope* to evaluate against. Without confirmed requirements, the candidate enumerates every plausible noun, including ones outside the scope they didn't establish. The recovery has to go back to Step 1 first — establish what's in scope (probably: vehicle types, spot assignment, full-lot handling, exit) and what's out (probably: reservations, permits, subscribers, sensors, displays, payments). Then prune. Option B fixes the symptom (too many entities) without addressing the cause (no scope). Option C softens the recovery dangerously. Option D denies the failure.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, after this interviewer feedback, say: 'You're right — I skipped requirements. Let me back up. Quick clarifiers: payment in scope? Reservations? Multi-floor lots? Sensor-driven occupancy or manual? Once I have those confirmed, I'll prune the entity list. My guess is half of the 14 are out of scope for a standard lot.'
questions.proTip: Speculative entity lists are the visible failure of a skipped Requirements step. When you see one (in your own work or someone else's), the fix is always to go back to Step 1 first, then re-derive entities from the confirmed scope.

questions.question: Two candidates implement makeMove. Candidate M includes inline state mutations: `if (winner) gameState = WON; lastMoveTime = now; consecutiveTurns[player]++;`. Candidate N goes through a single `mutateState(transition)` method that takes a transition object. Both look fine in implementation. At extensibility time, the interviewer asks 'how would you add an audit log of all state changes?' Why does N's design absorb this trivially while M's requires hunting through every method?
options: N's design routes every mutation through one chokepoint, so adding `auditLog.append(transition)` inside `mutateState` instantly captures every state change everywhere — without touching makeMove or any other method. M's design has mutations scattered, so auditing requires finding each one and adding a hook beside it. The implementation-time choice (chokepoint vs scattered) directly determines the cost of an extension-time feature (audit). This is why the framework grades extensibility on *what existing boundaries enable*, not on *what features were anticipated*.
Both designs absorb it equally — audit log is just a logger added to the project, not a structural concern about where mutations happen, and either design can route logs to the same external system.

M's design is actually easier to audit because the inline mutations are visible and auditable in place; N's `mutateState` indirection hides the actual mutation, making auditing harder to verify is correct because it's one level removed from the actual change.

N's design is over-engineered for the original requirement (no audit was asked for at implementation time), so even though it's easier to extend now, it lost points at implementation for unnecessary indirection that wasn't justified by the spec.
questions.explanation: Single-chokepoint mutation is the structural choice that makes an entire class of extensions trivial. Audit log: one `auditLog.append` inside mutateState. Undo: a stack of transitions, popped through the same method. Replay: the same. Observability metrics: same. M's scattered design pays the cost of every one of those extensions multiplied by the number of mutation sites. The framework's insight ('This works because you've isolated state mutations') generalizes far beyond undo. Option B denies the connection. Option C inverts (visibility is not auditability). Option D treats N's chokepoint as over-engineering — it isn't, because the design isn't speculative-extension; it's a *cleaner present design* that happens to also enable extensions.
questions.interviewScript: In your interview, say: 'N's chokepoint makes audit trivial — one line inside mutateState captures everything. M's scattered design needs N hooks for N mutation sites, plus the maintenance burden of keeping them in sync. The chokepoint isn't extra design for extensibility; it's a cleaner *present* design that happens to make audit, undo, and replay one-line additions later. That's the relationship between Class Design discipline and Extensibility — you don't have to anticipate the extension; you just have to keep mutations isolated.'
questions.proTip: Mutation-chokepoint isn't an extensibility pattern — it's a Class Design discipline that incidentally enables many extensions. The discipline pays for itself even with no extension; the extensions are a bonus.
